questions.q: What everyday substance did the British monopolize and tax, making it a symbol of colonial injustice?
questions.explanation: The British monopoly and tax on salt made this daily necessity a symbol of colonial injustice.

questions.q: From what source did Indians make salt during the civil disobedience protests?
questions.explanation: Indians made salt from seawater to defy the British salt monopoly.

questions.q: Why was salt an especially powerful symbol for mass protest?
options: It was a daily necessity used by everyone
It was only used in temples
questions.explanation: Salt was a daily necessity used by all Indians, rich and poor, making its tax a universal grievance.

questions.q: Which leader turned salt into an emblem of mass civil disobedience in 1930?
questions.explanation: Gandhi turned salt into an emblem of mass civil disobedience with the 1930 Salt March.

questions.q: By making their own salt, Indians were defying what kind of British control?
questions.explanation: Making their own salt defied the British monopoly and tax on salt production.

questions.q: Breaking the salt law was a form of which Gandhian method of struggle?
questions.explanation: Breaking the salt law was an act of nonviolent civil disobedience, the heart of Gandhi's method.

questions.q: The salt protests of 1930 launched which major movement?
questions.explanation: The salt protests of 1930 launched the Civil Disobedience Movement.

questions.q: The salt issue is most associated with the famous march to which village?
questions.explanation: The salt protest is most associated with Gandhi's march to the coastal village of Dandi.

questions.q: In which year did the salt-tax defiance become a nationwide movement?
questions.explanation: The salt-tax defiance became a nationwide movement in 1930.

questions.q: What did Indians turn an ordinary substance like salt into during the protests?
options: An emblem of mass civil disobedience
questions.explanation: Indians turned salt, an ordinary substance, into an emblem of mass civil disobedience.

questions.q: The British salt monopoly meant Indians were legally barred from doing what?
options: Producing or selling salt without paying the tax
questions.explanation: The salt monopoly made it illegal for Indians to produce or sell salt without paying the government tax.

questions.q: Which Salt Act consolidated the British government's monopoly over salt in India?
questions.explanation: The Salt Act of 1882 consolidated the British government's monopoly over the manufacture and sale of salt.

questions.q: Why did the salt tax hit the poorest Indians hardest?
options: Salt was essential to all and the flat tax burdened the poor disproportionately
The poor produced all the salt
questions.explanation: Because salt is essential to everyone, the flat tax fell disproportionately on the poor who could least afford it.

questions.q: After 1930, salt-making defiance spread to coastal sites across India in the form of what?
questions.explanation: Salt satyagrahas and nonviolent salt raids spread to coastal regions across India after 1930.

questions.q: The boycott accompanying the salt campaign also targeted which imported British product?
questions.explanation: The salt campaign was accompanied by a boycott of foreign cloth and other British goods.

questions.q: Participation in the salt satyagraha was notable for the large involvement of which group?
questions.explanation: The salt satyagraha drew unusually large participation from women across the country.

questions.q: Salt produced illegally during the campaign was often used in what symbolic way?
options: Sold or auctioned publicly to flout the law
questions.explanation: Illegally made salt was often sold or auctioned publicly as an open act of defiance against the law.

questions.q: In southern India, who led a notable salt march to Vedaranyam as part of the salt campaign?
questions.explanation: C. Rajagopalachari led a salt march to Vedaranyam on the Tamil Nadu coast during the salt campaign.

questions.q: The salt tax was one of the grievances partly addressed by which 1931 agreement?
questions.explanation: The 1931 Gandhi-Irwin Pact allowed coastal communities to make salt for personal use, easing the salt grievance.

questions.q: What concession on salt did the Gandhi-Irwin Pact grant?
options: Total abolition of the salt tax
Government salt factories handed to villagers
The right of people near the coast to make salt for their own use
questions.explanation: The pact permitted people living near the coast to make salt for their own consumption, a limited concession.

questions.q: Why was making salt seen as an act anyone could participate in?
options: It required only seawater and the sun
questions.explanation: Salt could be made simply by evaporating seawater, so ordinary people everywhere could join the defiance.

questions.q: The salt protests demonstrated which broader principle of Gandhi's strategy?
options: That breaking an unjust law nonviolently could mobilize millions
questions.explanation: The salt protests showed how nonviolently breaking an unjust law could mobilize millions in mass struggle.

questions.q: India's Interim Government moved to abolish the salt tax in 1946, with the change taking effect in which year?
questions.explanation: The Interim Government decided to abolish the salt tax in October 1946, and the abolition took effect on 1 April 1947.

questions.q: The British salt revenue was so significant that salt was sometimes guarded by what physical barrier in earlier decades?
options: The Inland Customs Line, a great hedge
questions.explanation: In the nineteenth century the British maintained an Inland Customs Line, a vast hedge, partly to enforce the salt tax.

questions.q: Which prominent woman led the nonviolent raid on the Dharasana Salt Works during the salt campaign?
questions.explanation: Sarojini Naidu led the nonviolent raid on the Dharasana Salt Works after Gandhi's arrest in 1930.

questions.q: An American journalist's harrowing report on the Dharasana salt raid drew global attention; who was he?
questions.explanation: Webb Miller's eyewitness report of the brutal Dharasana salt raid brought the salt campaign worldwide attention.

questions.q: Roughly what share of British Indian government revenue did salt tax historically contribute at its peak?
options: More than half of all revenue
A small but politically symbolic share
questions.explanation: By 1930 the salt tax was a small share of revenue but its burden on the poor made it a potent symbolic grievance.

questions.q: Kamaladevi Chattopadhyay is remembered for what salt-related act during the campaign?
options: Selling contraband salt and inviting arrest
questions.explanation: Kamaladevi Chattopadhyay openly sold illegally made salt and courted arrest as part of the salt satyagraha.

questions.q: The salt monopoly is sometimes traced back to revenue policies first systematized under whose colonial administration?
options: The East India Company's Bengal administration
questions.explanation: Salt revenue controls were systematized under the East India Company's Bengal administration before the 1882 Salt Act formalized them.

questions.q: Why did Gandhi consider the salt tax a particularly clear example of an 'unjust law'?
options: It taxed a free gift of nature essential to all life
It applied only to the wealthy
questions.explanation: Gandhi argued the salt tax was unjust because it taxed a free gift of nature that was essential to all human life.
